setupPhase.phaseDescription: The group is preparing before any long campaign begins.
setupPhase.whatTheGroupIsWorkingOn: Getting everyone connected, choosing the lightest useful tools, and preparing for a short tutorial adventure.
setupPhase.nextGroupTask: Complete Session −1 tech and account setup.

roadmap.title: Session −1
roadmap.summary: Tech check, Discord, accounts, and basic tool setup.

roadmap.title: Session 0
roadmap.summary: Characters, table rules, tone, and group expectations.

roadmap.title: First Adventure
roadmap.summary: A short tutorial story where we learn by playing.

roadmap.title: The Real Campaign
roadmap.summary: Once the group is comfortable, the larger story begins.

sessionMinusOne.title: Session −1: Get Connected Before the Adventure
sessionMinusOne.whatThisIs: This is the practical setup session before Session 0. We are making sure everyone can hear each other, access the tools, and understand the basic flow before we worry about adventure stuff.
techChecklist: Discord username shared with the group privately
D&D Beyond account created if the DM chooses D&D Beyond for sheets or rules
Character Builder opened successfully in the chosen sheet tool
VTT decision still pending, if applicable
questions: Can you hear and speak in Discord?
Have you made a D&D Beyond account if the DM asks for one?
Do you need help creating a character?
What parts of D&D are still confusing?
sessionMinusOne.privacyNote: Do not post Discord handles or private contact details on this public site. Track those in Discord or private notes.

sessionZero.title: Session 0: Build the Party Before the Story Begins
sessionZero.intro: Session 0 is where the group agrees on characters, tone, table rules, boundaries, and expectations before the story starts.
topics: How serious, funny, dark, or chaotic do we want the game to be?
How do our characters know each other?
Why do our characters stay together?
What house rules are already active?
What kind of characters does the group need?
What will the first adventure be?
sessionZero.callout: Your character does not need to be best friends with everyone else. Your character does need a reason to cooperate, remain loyal to the group, and not sabotage the adventure.

techCheck.recommendedBrowserDeviceNotes: Use the device you expect to play on. Test microphone, speakers, browser, and any chosen sheet or VTT tool before game night.

vtt.title: VTT & Game-Night Platform Comparison
vtt.subtitle: Maps, minis, and virtual tabletops
vtt.intro: We are not looking for the flashiest virtual tabletop. We are looking for the lowest-friction setup that lets the DM run D&D, lets new players join without a technical scavenger hunt, and keeps Dungeons Hub as the place everyone starts on game night. Discord handles conversation. D&D Beyond may handle character sheets and rules. A VTT should only handle maps and combat when a map actually helps.
vtt.philosophy: A VTT is optional technology for sharing maps, tokens, dice, and tabletop scenes online. We are not choosing a VTT because it has the most features. We are choosing the lightest setup that helps the DM run the game and helps players understand where things are.
vtt.decisionRule: Choose the lightest tool that helps the DM run the game without turning preparation, setup, or troubleshooting into a second hobby.
vtt.priceNote: Prices are included for quick comparison. They can change, so confirm current plans, module costs, access rules, and subscriptions through the linked product pages before anyone spends money.

decision.groupRole: Test tools, be patient, and give feedback.

required: A shared map when location matters
A battle floor plan when a fight needs one

platforms.name: D&D Beyond
platforms.primaryRole: Character sheets, rules reference, and character builder
platforms.costModel: Free account available. Hero Tier from $2.99/mo or $2.17/mo billed annually; Master Tier from $5.99/mo or $4.58/mo billed annually. Books are sold separately.
platforms.bestUse: Primary character-sheet and rules destination if the DM chooses that workflow.

platforms.name: D&D Beyond Maps
platforms.costModel: Players can use D&D Beyond with a free account. Master Tier from $5.99/mo or $4.58/mo billed annually unlocks stronger Maps VTT features such as custom map uploads; books are sold separately.
platforms.bestUse: Worth testing because the group may already use D&D Beyond, but it is not the default VTT choice.

platforms.name: Discord
platforms.primaryRole: Voice chat, text chat, scheduling, and announcements
platforms.costModel: Free base service. Optional Nitro upgrades are commonly around $2.99/mo to $9.99/mo and are not required for this group.
platforms.bestUse: Core communication layer before, during, and after sessions.

platforms.name: Fantasy Grounds Unity
platforms.primaryRole: Full-featured desktop VTT with automation and licensed modules
platforms.costModel: Fantasy Grounds VTT software is currently free to use. Licensed rules modules, adventures, art subscriptions, and add-ons may still cost extra.
platforms.bestUse: Possible future option if the group wants deep automation and accepts more setup.

platforms.name: Foundry VTT
platforms.primaryRole: Browser VTT with extensive modules and customization
platforms.costModel: $50 one-time host software license. Players join through a browser. Hosting services, premium modules, or paid content may cost extra.
platforms.bestUse: Strong future-growth option, but probably too much for the first tutorial adventure.

platforms.name: Owlbear Rodeo
platforms.primaryRole: Lightweight browser maps, tokens, fog, and drawing tools
platforms.costModel: Free base tier available. Paid plans are commonly around $3.99/mo and $7.99/mo for more storage, rooms, or support.
platforms.bestUse: Best current fit for quick maps, combat floors, low prep, and beginner access.

platforms.name: Roll20
platforms.primaryRole: Browser VTT with maps, tokens, sheets, dice, marketplace, and automation
platforms.costModel: Free base tier available. Plus is commonly about $5.99/mo or $59.99/yr; Pro is commonly about $9.99/mo or $109.99/yr. Marketplace content costs extra.
platforms.bestUse: Useful backup if the group wants a more established all-in-one browser VTT.

platforms.name: Tabletop Simulator
platforms.primaryRole: Steam-based 3D physics tabletop sandbox with Workshop content
platforms.costModel: $19.99 per copy; $59.99 four-pack. Optional DLC and Workshop assets vary.
platforms.bestUse: Best for a digital minis-and-terrain experience, not a browser-first site-centered setup.

platforms.name: Theater of the Mind + Discord
platforms.primaryRole: DM narration with maps used only when truly necessary
platforms.costModel: No VTT cost. Discord free account is sufficient.
platforms.bestUse: Excellent baseline for roleplay-heavy scenes, travel, exploration, and early learning.

candidates.whatItIsFor: The DM describes scenes verbally. A map appears only when it would genuinely help.
candidates.bestFor: Roleplay, travel, exploration, simple encounters, and low-prep games.
candidates.limits: Players may have less visual clarity during complex combat.
candidates.costNotes: No VTT cost. Discord free account is enough.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It keeps technology from becoming the game.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: Complex tactical fights may need a visual aid.

candidates.bestFor: Simple online maps without a heavy campaign platform.
candidates.limits: Character sheets and campaign management live elsewhere.
candidates.costNotes: Free base tier. Paid plans are commonly around $3.99/mo and $7.99/mo for more storage, rooms, or support.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It may be enough when the group only needs a map layer.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: It may not satisfy groups that want deeper automation.

candidates.whatItIsFor: A fuller virtual tabletop with maps, tokens, dice, and sheet support.
candidates.bestFor: Groups that want more VTT features in one place.
candidates.limits: More features can mean more setup and more interface to learn.
candidates.costNotes: Free base tier. Plus is commonly about $5.99/mo or $59.99/yr; Pro is commonly about $9.99/mo or $109.99/yr. Marketplace content costs extra.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It can centralize more of the tabletop.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: It may be heavier than the first adventure needs.

candidates.whatItIsFor: A D&D Beyond map option if the table also uses that ecosystem.
candidates.bestFor: Groups already using D&D Beyond for sheets and rules.
candidates.limits: It should not be chosen only because it is attached to a character-sheet tool.
candidates.costNotes: D&D Beyond has a free account tier. Master Tier starts at $5.99/mo or $4.58/mo billed annually for stronger Maps VTT features; books are sold separately.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It may reduce account sprawl if D&D Beyond is already chosen.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: The DM may prefer a different map workflow.

candidates.whatItIsFor: A customizable browser VTT with a large module ecosystem.
candidates.bestFor: Groups that want a powerful long-term VTT and have someone willing to own setup and hosting.
candidates.limits: The customization that makes it strong can also create more setup work than a tutorial adventure needs.
candidates.costNotes: $50 one-time host software license. Hosting services, premium modules, or paid content may cost extra.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It can grow with a longer campaign if the DM wants that level of control.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: It is probably not the lightest first-session tool.

candidates.whatItIsFor: A full-featured desktop VTT with automation and licensed modules.
candidates.bestFor: Groups that want deep automation and are comfortable installing and learning a heavier platform.
candidates.limits: It adds more setup burden than a browser-first beginner path.
candidates.costNotes: Fantasy Grounds VTT software is currently free to use. Paid rules modules, adventures, art subscriptions, and add-ons may still apply.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It may be useful later if the table wants a more automated desktop VTT.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: It is too heavy for the current group-prep phase.

candidates.whatItIsFor: A 3D tabletop sandbox for digital boards, pieces, and scenes.
candidates.bestFor: Groups that specifically want a 3D tabletop feel.
candidates.limits: It can become a technology project instead of a beginner aid.
candidates.costNotes: $19.99 per copy; $59.99 four-pack. Optional DLC and Workshop assets vary.
candidates.whyItMayFit: It can feel like moving pieces around a real table.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: It is probably more setup than a first tutorial adventure needs.

candidates.whatItIsFor: Any other tool the DM decides is easier for the actual game.
candidates.limits: The group should not force a tool the DM does not want to run.
candidates.whyItMayFit: The best tool is the one the DM can actually use comfortably.
candidates.whyItMayNotFit: Players may need to learn something unexpected.

rows.criterion: Players can use it in a browser
No installed app required for every player
Can show shared maps and battle floor plans
Low DM prep for map-only-when-needed play
Supports improvisation and Rule of Cool play
Works alongside Discord instead of replacing it
Fits a D&D Beyond character-sheet workflow if chosen
Still works well when no map is needed
Can plausibly become a Dungeons Hub widget
Easy to test and abandon after one tutorial adventure
Recommended use for this group right now

vtt.workingRecommendation: The cleanest starting setup is Dungeons Hub + Discord + D&D Beyond for character sheets if the DM chooses it + Theater of the Mind, with Owlbear Rodeo added only when a battle map or floor plan will make a scene clearer. That gives the DM room to improvise, keeps player setup low, avoids forcing everyone into a paid installed application, and preserves a simple exit path if the group later decides it wants Roll20, Foundry, Tabletop Simulator, or something else.

mapFaq.question: Do we need a map for everything?
mapFaq.answer: No. Most scenes can be described through roleplay and narration. A world map may help show where the party is traveling. A battle map or floor plan is mainly useful when movement, distance, cover, or positioning matters during a fight.

mapFaq.question: Will the DM need to build every town and house?
mapFaq.answer: No. The DM can describe a town without constructing it. Digital maps, paper maps, miniatures, floor plans, or no map at all can all work depending on the scene.

mapFaq.question: What is a battle map?
mapFaq.answer: A battle map is a top-down view of a location used when positioning matters. It may show a room, road, forest clearing, tavern, bridge, cave, or other encounter area.

mapFaq.question: What is theater of the mind?
mapFaq.answer: Theater of the mind means the group imagines the scene from the DM's description instead of using a detailed map or miniatures.

firstAdventure.title: Our First Adventure
firstAdventure.purpose: Use a short adventure to learn the table, rules, characters, and tools before beginning a longer campaign.
firstAdventure.whyThisWasChosen: A short tutorial adventure gives new players room to learn without committing the group to a long campaign immediately.
firstAdventure.notesForNewPlayers: This is a learning adventure. The goal is not to play perfectly; it is to learn how our table works.
firstAdventure.groupNotes: Dragons of Stormwreck Isle is a possible tutorial adventure, but the DM has not confirmed the final choice.
